findings.title: Button element missing keyboard event handler
findings.description: The submit button in login.html has an onclick handler but no onKeyDown or onKeyUp handler. While the button element is natively keyboard accessible, the JavaScript function goToGallery() is only bound to the click event. If a user presses Enter on a focused button, the onclick fires automatically, but this should be explicitly tested. However, form submission via Enter on inputs is a keyboard accessibility issue.
findings.fix_suggestion: Change the button type from 'button' to 'submit' and wrap the input in a form element. This ensures native Enter key submission: <form onsubmit="(e) => { e.preventDefault(); goToGallery(); }"><input id="gallery-slug" type="text" /><button type="submit">Continue</button></form>
findings.evidence: <button type="button" onclick="goToGallery()">Continue</button>
findings.impact: Keyboard-only users may experience inconsistent behavior if form submission is expected but the button is not properly wired to keyboard events.

findings.title: Form submission not keyboard accessible in feedback.html
findings.description: The submit button uses onclick handler. While the button element is keyboard accessible, the form cannot be submitted by pressing Enter in the textarea or input fields without a wrapping form element with onsubmit handler.
findings.fix_suggestion: Wrap form inputs in a <form> element with onsubmit handler: <form id="feedback-form" onsubmit="(e) => { e.preventDefault(); submitFeedback(); }">...fields...</form> and change button type to 'submit'.
findings.evidence: <button id="fb-submit" type="button" onclick="submitFeedback()">Submit report</button>
findings.impact: Users relying on keyboard navigation expect to press Enter in a textarea or final input to submit a form. Without a proper form element, this standard interaction pattern breaks.

findings.title: Missing skip-to-main link in demo.html
findings.description: The demo.html page has a site navigation with multiple links but no skip-to-main-content link. The skip link should be the first focusable element on the page to allow keyboard users to bypass the navigation and jump directly to the main content.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add a skip link as the first element in the header: Skip to main content and add id="main-content" to the main div or section where form begins.
findings.evidence: <header class="site-nav" id="site-nav">..nav links..</header>
findings.impact: Keyboard users must tab through all navigation links before reaching main content, increasing cognitive load and reducing efficiency.

findings.title: Focus order unclear due to programmatic DOM manipulation
findings.description: gallery.html uses display: none to hide/show screens (form-screen vs success-screen, galleries-view vs gallery-view). When display toggles from none to block, focus may not be managed properly. Keyboard users might not know where focus landed after screen transitions.
findings.fix_suggestion: After toggling visibility, programmatically move focus to the primary heading or first interactive element. Example: document.getElementById('gallery-view').classList.add('visible'); document.getElementById('gallery-title')?.focus(); Add tabindex="-1" to elements that should receive focus.
findings.evidence: #gallery-view { display: none; } #gallery-view.visible { display: block; }
findings.impact: When modals, screens, or overlays appear/disappear, keyboard users lose track of focus. Screen readers don't announce which element received focus after the transition.

findings.title: Images in gallery.html photo grid may lack alt text
findings.description: The photo grid in gallery.html dynamically loads images from an API. The img elements are created without explicit alt attributes: tags are added to .photo-item containers but no alt text is provided. If photos are purely decorative, alt="" is correct. If they are content (which they are in a gallery), they need descriptive alt text.
findings.fix_suggestion: Inject alt text when creating image elements. The alt should describe the photo content (e.g., the photo title, shot type, or description from metadata). Example: const img = document.createElement('img'); img.src = photoUrl; img.alt = photo.title || 'Gallery photo'; container.appendChild(img);
findings.evidence: .photo-item img { } [dynamically inserted via JS without alt attribute]
findings.impact: Screen reader users cannot understand the content or purpose of the gallery photos. The gallery is unusable without seeing the images or having textual descriptions.

findings.title: Input placeholder used as label substitute
findings.description: Multiple forms rely on placeholder attributes as the only label hint. In login.html, feedback.html, and demo.html, inputs like <input placeholder="joanne-garrison" /> or <input placeholder="example@email.com" /> lack explicit <label> elements.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add explicit <label> tags and keep placeholder for hints only. Example: <label for="gallery-slug">Gallery slug</label><input id="gallery-slug" placeholder="joanne-garrison" />. Placeholder should reinforce the label, not replace it.
findings.evidence: <input id="gallery-slug" autocomplete="off" placeholder="joanne-garrison" />
findings.impact: When users focus on an input, the placeholder text disappears, leaving users uncertain about what to enter. Screen readers may not properly announce the input purpose.

findings.title: Focus outline may lack sufficient visibility
findings.description: gallery.html includes *:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid var(--accent); } where --accent is #c4944a. On the dark background (#050505 or #0a0a0a), the contrast between #c4944a and #050505 is approximately 5.8:1, which meets the 3:1 threshold but is on the border of being hard to see for low-vision users.
findings.fix_suggestion: Enhance the focus indicator with a semi-transparent background or double outline: *:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid var(--accent); outline-offset: 2px; box-shadow: 0 0 0 4px rgba(196, 148, 74, 0.15); } This adds a halo effect for better visibility.
findings.evidence: *:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid var(--accent); outline-offset: 2px; }
findings.impact: Users with low vision may struggle to see the focus indicator, especially on complex or high-contrast backgrounds.

findings.title: Form validation error messages not always associated with inputs
findings.description: In demo.html, form errors are shown via showFieldError() which adds class 'has-error' and updates .field-error text. However, there's no explicit aria-describedby or aria-invalid attribute to link the error message to the input.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aria-invalid and aria-describedby to the input when an error occurs: const input = group.querySelector('input'); input.setAttribute('aria-invalid', 'true'); input.setAttribute('aria-describedby', `${field}-error`); errEl.id = `${field}-error`;
findings.evidence: function showFieldError(field, msg) { const group = document.getElementById(`fg-${field}`); group.classList.add('has-error'); }
findings.impact: Screen reader users may not know that an input has an error or what the error message is, since the error is not programmatically associated with the input.

findings.title: Low contrast on muted text in dark theme
findings.description: Throughout the codebase, --text-muted (#5c5347) is used on --bg-deep (#050505). The contrast ratio is approximately 3.2:1, which fails the AA standard of 4.5:1 for normal text. This affects body copy in cards, descriptions, and secondary text.
findings.fix_suggestion: Increase contrast by lightening --text-muted from #5c5347 to #8a7f74 or darkening the background. Retest contrast: #8a7f74 on #050505 = 6.8:1, which meets AA. Update :root { --text-muted: #8a7f74; }
findings.evidence: color: var(--text-muted); [#5c5347 on #050505]
findings.impact: Users with low vision or reading difficulties struggle to read secondary text, descriptions, and helper text. Content is harder to scan.

findings.title: Label text not included in accessible name for some buttons
findings.description: In gallery.html, style-switcher pill buttons show labels like 'Vintage', 'High Contrast', etc. but don't have aria-label. The text is visible, so the button's accessible name is derived from the text content, which is correct. However, the button in admin mode (admin-btn) may not have clear labels.
findings.fix_suggestion: For buttons without visible text or icon-only buttons, add aria-label. For style pills, the visible text is sufficient. Example: <button class="style-pill" aria-label="Apply Vintage style">Vintage</button>
findings.evidence: .style-pill { } [contains visible text like 'Vintage']
findings.impact: Button purpose is clear from visible text, so this is low priority. However, for consistency and to ensure all interactive elements have explicit accessible names, aria-label could be added.

findings.title: Gallery card hover state may cause unexpected context change
findings.description: In gallery.html, .gallery-card:hover { transform: translateY(-4px); } applies a visual hover effect. This is not a context change (no navigation or focus event triggers). However, the card itself is clickable, and clicking navigates to the gallery. The hover is purely visual.
findings.fix_suggestion: No fix needed. The hover effect is visual only and does not trigger any context changes. Consider adding focus state styling for keyboard users: .gallery-card:focus-visible { outline: ... }
findings.evidence: .gallery-card:hover { transform: translateY(-4px); }
findings.impact: This is not a violation but a note on behavior. Hover effects should not cause unexpected focus changes. The current implementation is fine.

findings.title: Potential duplicate or missing ID attributes
findings.description: In demo.html and gallery.html, elements are dynamically created via JavaScript. If IDs are generated without ensuring uniqueness (e.g., creating multiple elements with id='item-1'), this violates the parsing requirement of WCAG 4.1.1.
findings.fix_suggestion: Ensure all dynamically generated IDs are unique. Use timestamps or counters: const uniqueId = `element-${Date.now()}-${Math.random()}`; or use data attributes instead of IDs for JS hooks: data-field="name" instead of id="fb-name".
findings.evidence: <div class="form-group" id="fg-name">
findings.impact: If the page creates duplicate IDs dynamically, HTML parsing becomes ambiguous. Screen readers may reference the wrong element. This is a potential issue dependent on runtime behavior.

findings.title: Focus indicators removed by outline: none without replacement
findings.file: dashboard/ada.html, dashboard/admin.html, dashboard/backoffice.html, dashboard/seo.html, dashboard/compliance.html, dashboard/product.html, dashboard/self-audit.html, dashboard/qa.html
findings.description: Multiple dashboard HTML files use CSS `outline: none;` on interactive elements without providing an equivalent visual focus indicator. While some have `border-color` changes on focus, this is insufficient for keyboard navigation accessibility. The outline property removal must be replaced with explicit focus-visible styling that provides clear visual feedback.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add explicit focus-visible styling with visible outline or box-shadow. Example: `.filter-bar select:focus, .filter-bar input:focus { outline: 2px solid var(--accent); outline-offset: 2px; }` or use box-shadow for design consistency.
findings.evidence: .filter-bar select:focus, .filter-bar input:focus { outline: none; border-color: var(--accent); }
findings.impact: Keyboard-only users cannot identify which element has focus during navigation. This creates a significant barrier for users unable to use a mouse or touchpad. The subtle border-color change may not be visible in all contexts.

findings.title: Potential positive tabindex values could disrupt focus order
findings.description: While the current codebase appears to avoid explicit positive tabindex values, interactive elements rely on default focus order. The complex layouts with CSS grid and flexbox could result in visual vs. logical focus order mismatches, especially in responsive layouts. This requires verification across all interactive elements.
findings.fix_suggestion: Audit focus order by tabbing through each page with keyboard only. Verify tab order matches visual left-to-right, top-to-bottom flow. If needed, explicitly set tabindex="0" on custom interactive elements that don't receive focus naturally.
findings.evidence: Multiple form inputs and buttons that rely on natural tab order in complex layout structures
findings.impact: Keyboard users may experience unexpected focus navigation patterns that don't match the visual layout, leading to confusion and slower navigation.

findings.title: Animation pulse effect lacks motion preference support
findings.description: The `.header-meta .live-dot` element uses a `pulse` animation that continuously loops with opacity and box-shadow changes. This animation does not respect the `prefers-reduced-motion` media query, which means users who have requested reduced motion for accessibility reasons (often due to vestibular disorders) will still see the animation.
findings.fix_suggestion: Wrap animation in @media query: `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { .header-meta .live-dot { animation: none; opacity: 1; } }`. Add this rule after the @keyframes definition.
findings.evidence: .header-meta .live-dot { animation: pulse 2s infinite; } @keyframes pulse { 0%, 100% { opacity: 1; box-shadow: 0 0 0 0 rgba(46,213,115,0.4); } 50% { opacity: 0.7; box-shadow: 0 0 0 6px rgba(46,213,115,0); } }
findings.impact: Users with vestibular disorders, migraines triggered by motion, or other motion-sensitive conditions experience discomfort. This violates their accessibility preferences at the OS/browser level.

findings.title: Form inputs may not meet minimum touch target size
findings.description: While the current dashboard appears to be primarily desktop-focused, form inputs use `padding: 0.45rem 0.75rem` which translates to approximately 28-32 CSS pixels for form element height. On mobile or when considering touch accessibility, this may fall below the recommended 44x44 pixel touch target minimum.
findings.fix_suggestion: For desktop, current size is acceptable. For mobile, add responsive padding: `@media (max-width: 640px) { .filter-bar input, .filter-bar select { padding: 0.75rem 1rem; min-height: 44px; } }`
findings.evidence: .filter-bar select, .filter-bar input { padding: 0.45rem 0.75rem; } and <input type="text" style="padding:0.6rem 0.7rem;">
findings.impact: Users with motor impairments, tremors, or those on mobile devices may have difficulty accurately clicking or tapping small form controls, leading to input errors and frustration.

findings.title: Language attribute present but limited to single language
findings.description: All dashboard files correctly include `<html lang="en">`, which is positive. However, if any embedded content or specific text sections use different languages (e.g., code snippets, technical terms, or internationalized content), they lack language override attributes. This is a minor issue for English-dominant dashboards but worth noting.
findings.fix_suggestion: If non-English content is added in future, wrap it with language attributes: `contenido`. This is informational/preventative.
findings.evidence: <html lang="en"> is present on all files
findings.impact: Screen readers use the correct English pronunciation rules for all content. No current impact, but future multilingual content would lack proper language markup.

findings.title: Tab-based interface lacks complete ARIA tab pattern
findings.description: The `.tab-btn` elements in ada.html function as tabs for switching between content panels (.tab-panel). The implementation is functional but lacks complete ARIA tab pattern attributes (role="tablist", role="tab", aria-selected, aria-controls). This may cause screen reader users to not understand the tab pattern structure.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Add ARIA attributes: `
...
...
`
findings.evidence: .tab-btn { ... cursor: pointer; } without role="tab", aria-selected, or aria-controls attributes. .tab-panel { display: none; } .tab-panel.active { display: block; }
findings.impact: Screen reader users cannot efficiently navigate between tabs and may not understand the relationship between tab buttons and their corresponding panels. Semantic meaning is lost.

findings.title: Unlock modal password field has no visible or programmatic label
findings.description: The unlock gate renders a required password input with only placeholder text. Placeholders are not a sufficient label and disappear as soon as the user starts typing.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add an associated label, for example: <label htmlFor="unlock-password">Password</label><input id="unlock-password" ... />
findings.evidence: <input type="password" ... placeholder="Your password" required autoFocus autoComplete="current-password" />
findings.impact: Screen reader users can land on an unnamed required field when trying to unlock encrypted journal content, which blocks a core flow.

findings.title: Settings current-password field is unlabeled
findings.description: The change-password form shows a required current-password input without a visible label or another accessible naming mechanism.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add a label tied to the field: <label htmlFor="current-password">Current password</label><input id="current-password" ... />
findings.evidence: <input type="password" value={currentPassword} ... placeholder="Current password" required autoComplete="current-password" />
findings.impact: Assistive technology users cannot reliably identify the first password field in the account password-change flow.

findings.title: Settings new-password field is unlabeled
findings.description: The second required password field also relies on placeholder text instead of a persistent label.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add a visible label and id pair, such as <label htmlFor="new-password">New password</label><input id="new-password" ... />
findings.evidence: <input type="password" value={newPassword} ... placeholder="New password (min 10 characters)" required minLength={10} autoComplete="new-password" />
findings.impact: Screen reader users can miss which field creates the replacement password, increasing the chance of failed password changes.

findings.title: Unlock modal is not exposed as a dialog
findings.description: The unlock overlay is visually modal, but the wrapper lacks dialog semantics such as role="dialog", aria-modal="true", and an accessible label reference.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Mark the container as a dialog and tie it to the heading, for example:
...

Unlock Journal
findings.evidence: <div className="fixed inset-0 z-50 flex items-center justify-center bg-black/60 px-4"><div className="w-full max-w-sm ...">
findings.impact: Screen reader users are not informed that focus has moved into a blocking dialog, making the locked state harder to understand and navigate.

findings.title: Encryption recovery overlay is not exposed as a dialog
findings.description: The password-recovery overlay uses fixed-position modal styling but does not provide dialog role/state information to assistive technology.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add role="dialog", aria-modal="true", and aria-labelledby tied to the modal heading. This modal should also move focus into the dialog on open.
findings.evidence: <div className="fixed inset-0 z-50 flex items-center justify-center bg-black/60 px-4"><div className="w-full max-w-sm ...">
findings.impact: Users of screen readers may continue exploring background content and miss the context switch into a recovery task.

findings.title: Recovery codes overlay is not exposed as a dialog
findings.description: The recovery-codes confirmation screen presents as a modal but does not identify itself as one to assistive technologies.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add dialog semantics: role="dialog", aria-modal="true", and aria-labelledby on the modal container, plus a heading id.
findings.evidence: <div className="fixed inset-0 z-50 flex items-center justify-center bg-black/60 px-4"><div className="w-full max-w-md ...">
findings.impact: Users can lose context while trying to complete a critical security step because the blocking overlay is not announced as a dialog.

findings.title: Journal editor labels are not associated with their fields
findings.description: The journal composer renders visible labels for Reflection, Prayer Requests, and Tags, but the labels have no htmlFor and the controls have no ids. The visible prompts are not programmatically tied to the controls.
findings.fix_suggestion: Assign ids to the textarea and input controls and connect each visible label with htmlFor, or use aria-labelledby where appropriate.
findings.evidence:
<label className="...">Reflection</label>
<textarea value={content} ... />
findings.impact: Screen reader users may hear generic "edit text" controls instead of the field purpose while creating or editing journal entries.

findings.title: Verse reflection editor labels are not associated with their controls
findings.description: The reflection form uses presentational labels for Title, Reference, Version, Verse, Prompt, Reflection, Prayer Requests, and Tags, but none of those labels are programmatically associated with the related inputs, textareas, or select.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add stable ids to each form control and convert each visible label into a proper label association with htmlFor.
findings.evidence:
<label className="...">Title</label>
<input value={title} ... />
findings.impact: Assistive technology users can lose track of which control edits which part of the reflection, making a primary authoring workflow difficult.

findings.title: Books browser accordion buttons do not expose expanded state
findings.description: Each book row is a custom disclosure button, but the toggle does not report aria-expanded or identify the controlled panel.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aria-expanded={expandedBook === book.bookid}, aria-controls for the chapter panel id, and assign that id to the revealed panel.
findings.evidence: <button onClick={() => { setExpandedBook(...); ... }} className="tap-target flex w-full ...">
findings.impact: Screen reader users cannot tell whether a book is collapsed or expanded before navigating into the chapter list.

findings.title: Reading plan disclosure buttons do not expose expanded state
findings.description: Plan cards are expanded and collapsed with custom buttons, but those controls do not announce whether the card is open or closed.
findings.fix_suggestion: Set aria-expanded={isExpanded}, aria-controls on the header button, and add the matching id to the expanded detail region.
findings.evidence: <button onClick={() => handleExpandPlan(plan.id)} className="tap-target flex w-full items-center gap-3 ...">
findings.impact: Users of assistive technology must guess whether additional plan details and progress controls are currently visible.

findings.title: History disclosure buttons do not expose expanded state
findings.description: The history screen uses custom toggle buttons for reflection and study cards without aria-expanded or a controlled-region relationship.
findings.fix_suggestion: Apply aria-expanded and aria-controls to each header button and give each expandable region a unique id.
findings.evidence: <button onClick={() => setExpandedId(isExpanded ? null : item.id)} className="flex w-full items-start justify-between gap-3 text-left">
findings.impact: Screen reader users cannot tell which cards are open, making it harder to review saved studies and reflections efficiently.

findings.title: Journal entry disclosure buttons do not expose expanded state
findings.description: Journal cards have custom expand/collapse controls, but those buttons do not communicate state to assistive technologies.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aria-expanded and aria-controls to the trigger buttons and an id to the expandable panel.
findings.evidence: <button onClick={() => setExpandedId(isExpanded ? null : entry.id)} className="flex-1 text-left">
findings.impact: Assistive technology users must explore the DOM to discover whether extra prayer-request and action content is present.

findings.title: Reflection disclosure buttons do not expose expanded state
findings.description: Saved reflection cards are expanded with custom buttons, but those controls lack aria-expanded and aria-controls.
findings.fix_suggestion: Expose disclosure state with aria-expanded and point aria-controls to the corresponding expanded content container.
findings.evidence: <button onClick={() => setExpandedId(expanded ? null : entry.id)} className="flex-1 text-left">
findings.impact: Screen reader users cannot reliably understand whether the verse, prompt, tags, and delete actions are currently visible.

findings.title: Study book search field relies on placeholder text instead of a label
findings.description: The book-search input inside the study passage picker has no label or aria-label, so its purpose depends entirely on placeholder text.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add a visible or screen-reader label, for example <label htmlFor="study-book-search" className="sr-only">Search books</label><input id="study-book-search" ... />
findings.evidence: <input type="text" value={bookSearch} ... placeholder="Search books..." ... autoFocus />
findings.impact: When the placeholder disappears, screen reader and speech-input users lose a reliable field name while filtering books.

findings.title: Study chat replies and loading status are not announced
findings.description: The follow-up chat appends assistant replies and a loading message to a generic scroll container without aria-live, role="status", or role="log".
findings.fix_suggestion: Expose the message list as a live log, for example <div role="log" aria-live="polite" aria-relevant="additions text">, and mark the loading line as role="status".
findings.evidence:
... {loading &&
Reflecting on your question...
}
findings.impact: Screen reader users may not hear when the app starts processing or when a new answer arrives, leaving the interaction silent and confusing.

findings.title: Login form omits autocomplete tokens for personal-data fields
findings.description: The sign-in form does not identify the email and password fields with appropriate autocomplete tokens.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add autoComplete="email" to the email field and autoComplete="current-password" to the password field.
findings.evidence:
<input id="email" type="email" ... required />
<input id="password" type="password" ... required />
findings.impact: Users who rely on browser autofill, password managers, or cognitive support features get less reliable form filling on a core auth path.

findings.title: Registration form omits autocomplete tokens for name, email, and password fields
findings.description: The create-account form does not expose input purpose for the personal-data fields used during registration.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use autoComplete="name", autoComplete="email", and autoComplete="new-password" on the registration inputs. The confirmation field should also use autoComplete="new-password".
findings.evidence:
<input id="name" type="text" ... />
<input id="email" type="email" ... />
<input id="password" type="password" ... />
findings.impact: People using autofill and assistive input tools get a slower and less reliable sign-up flow.

findings.title: Forgot-password email field omits autocomplete
findings.description: The reset-request form asks for an email address but does not identify it with an autocomplete token.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add autoComplete="email" to the email input.
findings.evidence: <input id="email" type="email" value={email} ... required />
findings.impact: Users miss browser assistance for a common recovery flow and may have to re-enter data manually.

findings.title: Reset-password form omits autocomplete tokens for email, code, and new-password fields
findings.description: The password-reset completion form does not expose the purpose of the email, one-time code, or new-password inputs.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add autoComplete="email", autoComplete="one-time-code", and autoComplete="new-password" to the relevant fields. The confirmation field should also use autoComplete="new-password".
findings.evidence:
<input id="email" type="email" ... />
<input id="code" type="text" inputMode="numeric" ... />
<input id="password" type="password" ... />
findings.impact: Password managers and platform autofill tools cannot help users complete the reset flow as reliably as they should.

findings.title: Inline links rely on color alone - insufficient contrast cue
findings.description: Paragraph text contains links styled only by color (var(--accent) #c4944a) with no underlines or other visual indicators. Text-only users cannot distinguish links from surrounding text. This violates WCAG 1.4.1 which requires information not be conveyed by color alone.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add underlines to links in main content areas: main a, .prose a { text-decoration: underline; text-underline-offset: 0.25em; }. Keep navigation and button links without underlines.
findings.evidence: a { color: var(--accent); text-decoration: none; } removes underlines globally. In body copy and paragraphs, links are only distinguished by color.
findings.impact: Users with color blindness or those viewing on monochrome displays cannot identify links. Users scanning content quickly may miss links. This affects 8% of males with color vision deficiency.

findings.title: Blog filter results not announced to screen readers
findings.description: When users filter blog posts by keyword, product, category, or tag, the results update dynamically but there is no aria-live region to announce the changes to screen reader users. Users relying on assistive technology will not know that the visible results have changed.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Add aria-live='polite' and role='status' to #blog-results-meta:
Showing {posts.length} posts
findings.evidence: The #blog-results-meta div updates via JavaScript (resultsCount.textContent = String(visibleCount)) but lacks aria-live or role='status'.
findings.impact: Screen reader users will not be notified when filtered results change, making the filtering feature completely unusable for them.

findings.title: Blog search input label inadequate for understanding field purpose
findings.description: The search input has only 'Keyword' as its label, with the actual description buried in placeholder text that disappears on focus. This violates WCAG 2.4.4 which requires link purpose to be clear. The label should describe what kind of search is performed.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use a more descriptive label:
findings.impact: Users with cognitive disabilities or those using form-filling assistants may not understand what the search field searches (keywords across blog titles, content, tags, etc.).

findings.title: Navigation dropdown menu may trap keyboard focus
findings.description: The navigation dropdown uses CSS :hover/:focus-within for visibility, but on mobile (< 960px) the menu becomes static with no keyboard management. When opened via keyboard, there's no focus trap protection, and users cannot close the menu with Escape key.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add JavaScript: (1) Move focus to first nav link when menu opens, (2) Handle Escape key to close menu, (3) Add aria-controls='nav-links' to toggle button and id='nav-links' to nav element.
findings.evidence: Desktop: .nav-dropdown-menu uses opacity and pointer-events controlled by :focus-within. Mobile: position becomes static and opacity/pointer-events are hardcoded, with no JS focus management visible.
findings.impact: Keyboard-only users on mobile may be unable to navigate out of the expanded menu or close it, effectively trapping them.

findings.title: Navigation toggle button missing aria-controls attribute
findings.description: The .nav-toggle button lacks aria-controls to indicate which element it controls. While it has aria-label and aria-expanded, the missing aria-controls breaks the semantic connection between the button and the menu.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aria-controls: <button class='nav-toggle' aria-label='Toggle menu' aria-expanded='false' aria-controls='nav-links'> and <nav class='nav-links' id='nav-links'>
findings.evidence: <button class='nav-toggle' aria-label='Toggle menu' aria-expanded='false'> missing aria-controls='nav-links'
findings.impact: Screen reader users won't understand which navigation element the toggle button controls, reducing clarity about menu functionality.

findings.title: Mobile navigation menu opens without moving focus
findings.description: When the mobile navigation menu opens (via nav-toggle), focus remains on the button rather than moving to the first menu item. This violates WCAG 2.4.3 best practices for focus order management.
findings.fix_suggestion: When .nav-links.open is applied, focus the first anchor tag: navLinks.querySelector('a')?.focus();
findings.evidence: The .nav-toggle button toggles .open class on .nav-links, but no visible JavaScript moves focus to the menu items.
findings.impact: Keyboard-only users must tab multiple times to reach the actual menu items, creating unnecessary friction.

findings.title: Blog filter form lacks instruction text explaining filter behavior
findings.description: The blog filtering form has three filters (Keyword, Product, Topic) but no text explaining how they work together (AND vs OR logic). Users, especially those with cognitive disabilities, may not understand the filtering mechanism.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Add:

Filter posts by selecting any combination. All selected filters must match:
findings.evidence: Filter labels are minimal (Keyword, Product, Topic). No instruction text explains that all selected filters must match (AND logic).
findings.impact: Users with cognitive disabilities or those unfamiliar with filtering UIs may be confused about expected filter behavior.

findings.title: Text spacing adjustments not verified for WCAG 1.4.12 compliance
findings.description: The site has not been verified to remain accessible when text spacing is adjusted per WCAG 1.4.12 (line-height 1.5x, letter-spacing 0.12em, word-spacing 0.16em, paragraph spacing 2x).
findings.fix_suggestion: Test the site at 200% zoom and with text spacing adjustments. Verify no horizontal scrolling or content loss occurs. Update CSS comments to confirm WCAG 1.4.12 compliance.
findings.evidence: Body uses line-height: 1.7 and responsive font sizing via clamp(), but max-width constraints and fixed container dimensions may cause overflow when spacing is increased.
findings.impact: Users who need increased text spacing (dyslexia, low vision) may experience content overflow or loss of readability.

findings.title: Button border contrast should be verified against secondary backgrounds
findings.description: Buttons use a 1px border in var(--accent) #c4944a, which should be tested for sufficient contrast against all background colors used throughout the site, including secondary backgrounds like #0f0f0f.
findings.fix_suggestion: Verify contrast ratios using WebAIM Contrast Checker. If below 3:1 against secondary backgrounds, increase border width to 2px or adjust color.
findings.evidence: .btn { border: 1px solid var(--accent); } where --accent is #c4944a. This needs 3:1 contrast ratio against all background colors.
findings.impact: Users with low vision or color blindness may have difficulty seeing button borders, making interactive elements less discoverable.

findings.title: Blog posts lack unique IDs for maintainability and accessibility
findings.description: Blog post elements use data-post-card attribute but lack unique id attributes. This can cause maintainability issues and prevent future developers from easily targeting specific posts.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add unique IDs: <a href={`/blog/${post.id}`} id='post-{post.id}' class='post-card fade-in' data-post-card ... >
findings.evidence: <a ... data-post-card ... > elements have no id='post-{post.id}' attribute. Multiple elements with only data attributes can lead to selector ambiguity.
findings.impact: Future code changes may fail silently. Automated accessibility testing tools may report issues due to missing IDs.

findings.title: External links lack visual indicator of opening in new window
findings.description: Links with target='_blank' (e.g., external product links in footer) lack visual indication that they open in a new window, potentially surprising users.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add CSS icon: a[target='_blank']::after { content: ' ↗'; font-size: 0.75em; margin-left: 0.25rem; }
findings.evidence: Cordivent has no visual indicator or text warning.
findings.impact: Users may be surprised when links open in new windows, disrupting their browsing flow. Users with cognitive disabilities may be confused.

findings.title: Blog search input should have autocomplete='off' for search queries
findings.description: The blog search input lacks an autocomplete attribute. While not required for non-personal-data fields, it's a best practice to explicitly set autocomplete='off' for search inputs.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add autocomplete='off': <input id='blog-search' type='search' name='q' autocomplete='off' ... />
findings.evidence: <input id='blog-search' type='search' name='q' ... /> missing autocomplete attribute
findings.impact: Low priority for current site. Browsers may suggest previous searches, which may not be desired for a content search.

findings.title: Navigation dropdown menu not dismissible with Escape key
findings.description: The dropdown menu uses CSS for visibility but has no JavaScript handler to close it when Escape is pressed. This violates best practices for accessible dropdown menus.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add: document.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => { if (e.key === 'Escape') { document.querySelectorAll('.nav-dropdown').forEach(d => d.classList.remove('open')); } });
findings.evidence: The .nav-dropdown-menu is controlled only by CSS :hover/:focus-within. No JavaScript Escape key handler is visible.
findings.impact: Keyboard-only users cannot dismiss the dropdown using the standard Escape key, requiring them to tab away or click elsewhere.

findings.title: Navigation links not keyboard accessible
findings.description: The primary navigation links use onclick handlers with href='#' instead of proper links. This creates keyboard accessibility issues as the elements don't respond properly to Enter/Space key activation through standard keyboard navigation patterns.
findings.fix_suggestion: Convert to proper button elements or use actual href links instead of onclick with hash hrefs
findings.evidence: Navigation anchor tags with onclick handlers instead of proper href routing
findings.impact: Keyboard-only users cannot navigate between views. Screen reader users may not understand the purpose of these elements.

findings.title: Modal overlay has focus trap without escape key handler
findings.description: The deployment confirmation modal lacks keyboard escape mechanism documented in code. Keyboard users may become trapped when the modal is displayed.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add keyboard event listener to handle Escape key for modal dismissal
findings.evidence: Modal dialog without documented Escape key handler or clear close button
findings.impact: Keyboard-only users may become trapped in the modal if they cannot find the Cancel button.

findings.title: Missing skip navigation link
findings.description: The page has a sticky navigation bar that remains visible but lacks a skip-to-main-content link. Users who tab through the interface must pass through all navigation items before reaching the main content.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add a skip link as the first focusable element on the page that links to main content with id='main-content'
findings.evidence: Navigation bar followed immediately by main content without skip link
findings.impact: Keyboard-only and screen reader users must navigate through all nav links to reach main content, significantly slowing access.

findings.title: Modal not properly marked with ARIA attributes
findings.description: The deployment confirmation modal lacks critical ARIA attributes (role='dialog', aria-modal='true', aria-labelledby) that inform assistive technologies about its purpose and semantic meaning.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add ARIA attributes: role='dialog', aria-modal='true', and aria-labelledby pointing to modal heading ID
findings.evidence: Modal div missing role, aria-modal, and aria-labelledby attributes
findings.impact: Screen reader users are not informed that this is a modal dialog and cannot be announced properly. Tab order and focus management are unclear.

findings.title: Form error messages rely on color alone
findings.description: The form error display uses only color (red background and text) with no text indicator or icon to convey error state. CSS class form-error only applies color styling without any supplementary text indicator.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add a visual error indicator such as an icon or text prefix like 'Error: ' before the error message text
findings.evidence: Form error div with only CSS color styling, no text prefix or icon
findings.impact: Users with color blindness cannot perceive that an error has occurred. Visual-only users still need non-color indicators.

findings.title: Focus indicator not visible on form inputs
findings.description: While input:focus has a box-shadow, the shadow (0 0 0 3px rgba(79,70,229,0.1)) is very subtle and may not meet the 3:1 contrast requirement for focus indicators.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use a more visible outline instead of box-shadow for focus state
findings.evidence: Input focus styling uses subtle box-shadow without explicit outline
findings.impact: Keyboard-only users may have difficulty seeing which form field is currently focused.

findings.title: Small touch target on modal buttons
findings.description: The modal buttons use the btn-sm class which reduces padding below recommended 44x44px minimum for touch targets.
findings.fix_suggestion: Ensure all buttons meet minimum 44x44px touch target size by adjusting btn-sm padding
findings.evidence: Modal buttons using btn-sm class with reduced padding
findings.impact: Mobile and touch-screen users may struggle to precisely click the buttons, especially the Cancel button.

findings.title: View switching does not manage focus
findings.description: The showView() function switches between sections but does not move focus to the newly visible section. When switching views, keyboard focus remains on the last focused element in the previous view.
findings.fix_suggestion: Move focus to the main heading of the new view after displaying it
findings.evidence: showView function hides/shows sections without moving focus
findings.impact: Keyboard users don't know where focus is after view switch. Screen reader users don't get announced the new view's content.

findings.title: Buttons may be created with divs and custom JavaScript
findings.description: Some interactive elements may be created as divs with onclick handlers rather than semantic button elements, particularly in dynamically generated content like agent groups.
findings.fix_suggestion: Ensure all interactive controls use semantic button elements
findings.evidence: Dynamic content generation patterns may bypass semantic button elements
findings.impact: Screen reader users cannot identify these as buttons. Keyboard users cannot activate them with Space key.

findings.title: Form label colors have marginal contrast
findings.description: Form labels use color: var(--text-muted) which is #718096. Against white background, this has approximately 4.5:1 contrast ratio, which meets AA but is at the threshold.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use darker color for labels to improve contrast to 7:1 or higher for better readability
findings.evidence: Form labels using text-muted color variable
findings.impact: Users with low vision or color blindness may have difficulty reading form labels.

findings.title: Back link text could be more descriptive
findings.description: The back link on the detail view uses an arrow character with text, but the arrow symbol should ideally have a text alternative or be part of the link text.
findings.fix_suggestion: Ensure link text is clear and includes descriptive text in addition to arrow symbol
findings.evidence: Back link with arrow character (larr entity)
findings.impact: Screen reader users will hear 'Back to deployments' but may be confused by arrow symbol if isolated.

findings.title: Required field indicator not documented
findings.description: Required fields are marked with a red asterisk but there is no descriptive text explaining that the asterisk indicates required fields.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add legend text at the top of form sections explaining that asterisks indicate required fields
findings.evidence: Multiple form fields with required asterisk span element
findings.impact: Users, especially those new to the form, may not understand that the asterisk indicates a required field.

findings.title: Dynamic content focus management
findings.description: When agent groups are dynamically added to the form, focus management and keyboard accessibility of new content may not be properly handled.
findings.fix_suggestion: Ensure dynamically added content maintains focus management and moves focus to new elements
findings.evidence: addAgentGroup function creates dynamic form elements
findings.impact: New form fields may not be keyboard accessible or properly announced to screen readers.

findings.title: Form changes trigger automatic updates without clear notification
findings.description: Several form inputs have onchange handlers that immediately update the form's visual state (updateZones, updateOsVersions, updateArchitecture). This could be unexpected for users.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aria-live regions to announce dynamic changes to assistive technology users
findings.evidence: Form selects with onchange handlers that trigger updateZones and similar functions
findings.impact: Screen reader and keyboard users may be surprised when selections change the form's visible content without explicit submission.

findings.title: Layout may not reflow properly at 200% zoom
findings.description: The container has a fixed max-width: 900px and form-row with minmax(200px, 1fr). At 200% zoom or 320px width, users may experience horizontal scrolling.
findings.fix_suggestion: Test at 320px and 200% zoom to ensure proper reflow, consider reducing grid minimum width
findings.evidence: Container with fixed max-width and form-row with minimum 200px
findings.impact: Users with low vision who zoom to 200% may experience horizontal scrolling in some viewport sizes.

findings.title: Help text not associated with form inputs
findings.description: Form fields have helpful descriptions in small tags, but these are not associated with inputs via aria-describedby attribute.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use aria-describedby attribute to associate help text with form inputs
findings.evidence: Form labels with small description tags below but no aria-describedby association
findings.impact: Screen reader users don't understand the relationship between the input and its help text description.

findings.title: Architecture preview decorative elements not marked
findings.description: The architecture preview sections (arch-preview div) are visual representations but have no alt text or semantic meaning marking them as decorative.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aria-hidden='true' to decorative previews, or provide text descriptions for screen readers
findings.evidence: arch-preview and agent-preview divs without aria-hidden or semantic markup
findings.impact: Screen reader users cannot understand the deployment topology being visualized.

findings.title: Modal close action not fully keyboard accessible
findings.description: The modal uses onclick on the overlay to detect clicks but keyboard activation may not properly trigger the close function.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add explicit close button or enhance JavaScript with keyboard event handling for Escape key
findings.evidence: Modal overlay with onclick handler using event.target check
findings.impact: Keyboard users relying on the overlay interaction may not succeed if focus is on the modal card.

findings.title: Table header scope attributes missing
findings.description: The deployments table has th elements but they lack scope attributes, making it harder for screen readers to properly announce row headers.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add scope='col' attribute to all th elements in table headers
findings.evidence: Table thead with th elements but no scope attributes
findings.impact: Screen reader users don't have clear understanding of which column header corresponds to each data cell.

findings.title: Status badge styling should include text
findings.description: Deployment status is shown with CSS class-based styling but text content may be the only indicator to screen readers.
findings.fix_suggestion: Ensure all status elements include actual text content in addition to CSS classes
findings.evidence: CSS classes for status badges without confirmation of text content
findings.impact: If CSS fails to load, status badges may be indistinguishable. Screen readers need actual text content.

findings.title: Output boxes may have text overflow issues
findings.description: The output-box class has overflow-x: auto. If users increase text spacing or font size, content may be truncated or require horizontal scrolling.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use word-break and white-space properties to allow wrapping instead of only horizontal scrolling
findings.evidence: Output box styling with font-size 0.85rem and overflow-x auto
findings.impact: Users with increased text spacing or low vision may have difficulty reading code output if it requires horizontal scrolling.

findings.title: Navigation links lack explicit focus indicator
findings.description: The nav-link elements don't have an explicit focus state defined in CSS. When tabbing through the nav, the focus state relies only on the browser default.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add :focus and :focus-visible CSS states to nav-link elements
findings.evidence: nav-link styling with hover and active states but no focus state
findings.impact: Keyboard-only users may not see which nav item has focus when tabbing.

findings.title: Login error message missing live region attributes
findings.description: The login-error paragraph uses display:none/block but doesn't have aria-live='assertive' to announce errors to screen readers.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add role='alert' and aria-live='assertive' to the login-error element
findings.evidence: Login error element without aria-live or role='alert'
findings.impact: Screen reader users don't get notified when login errors occur.

findings.title: Enhanced contrast ratios for AAA compliance (aspirational)
findings.description: While most colors meet AA contrast requirements, some secondary text like .text-muted labels are at the 4.5:1 threshold. For AAA compliance, aim for 7:1.
findings.fix_suggestion: Consider darker muted text color for AAA compliance aspiration
findings.evidence: CSS custom properties for text colors
findings.impact: Users with mild low vision would benefit from higher contrast throughout.

findings.title: Page language properly declared (compliant)
findings.description: The HTML element correctly declares lang='en', which is good for screen reader language selection.
findings.fix_suggestion: No fix needed - this is compliant.
findings.evidence: html lang='en' attribute
findings.impact: Positive finding - screen readers will use correct English pronunciation.

findings.title: Form submission is keyboard accessible (compliant)
findings.description: The form properly uses form element with button type='submit', which is fully keyboard accessible.
findings.fix_suggestion: No fix needed - this is compliant.
findings.evidence: Form with submit button
findings.impact: Positive finding - form submission is fully keyboard accessible.

findings.title: Modal dialogs not keyboard dismissible (UnlockModal, RecoveryCodesModal)
findings.description: Modal dialogs (UnlockModal at src/components/UnlockModal.tsx and RecoveryCodesModal at src/components/RecoveryCodesModal.tsx) do not close when Escape key is pressed, violating WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard and 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap. Users relying on keyboard navigation cannot dismiss these critical modals.
findings.impact: Keyboard-only users and screen reader users may become trapped in the modal without a way to dismiss it, unable to access the rest of the page.

findings.title: Modal dialogs missing proper ARIA attributes (role, aria-modal, aria-labelledby)
findings.description: UnlockModal and RecoveryCodesModal are missing critical ARIA attributes required by WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value. They lack `role="dialog"`, `aria-modal="true"`, and `aria-labelledby` attributes that identify the modal to assistive technologies.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Add proper ARIA attributes to the modal container:

Unlock Journal
findings.evidence: <div className="fixed inset-0 z-50 flex items-center justify-center bg-black/60 px-4">
findings.impact: Screen reader users may not recognize the modal as a dialog and may not understand the page structure. Modal heading and description may not be properly announced.

findings.title: Modal has no focus trap or focus management (UnlockModal, RecoveryCodesModal, PageGuide)
findings.description: Modal dialogs do not properly manage focus. When a modal opens, focus should be moved to the modal, and focus should be trapped within the modal (cannot Tab to elements outside). The PageGuide component implements focus trap correctly, but UnlockModal and RecoveryCodesModal do not.
findings.impact: Keyboard users can Tab out of the modal to background elements, violating focus management expectations and potentially allowing interaction with page elements that should be inert.

findings.title: Button uses title attribute instead of visible label (DemoRoleSelector)
findings.description: The DemoRoleSelector component at src/components/DemoRoleSelector.tsx uses `title` attribute for button descriptions instead of visible text labels. Title attributes are not reliably accessible to all users, violating WCAG 2.5.3 Label in Name.
findings.evidence:
<button
  key={r}
  onClick={() => setDemoRole(r)}
  aria-pressed={role === r}
  title={DEMO_ROLE_OPTIONS.find(option => option.role === r)?.summary}
  className={...}
>
findings.impact: Users without screen readers who rely on visible labels will not see the button descriptions, and screen reader users may not hear the title if they don't pause. The visible button text alone ("Coordinator", "Speaker", etc.) may not be sufficient context.

findings.title: UnlockModal input field has no visible label (accessibility issue)
findings.description: The password input in UnlockModal at src/components/UnlockModal.tsx has no associated `<label>` element. While placeholder text is present, placeholders should not serve as the only label. WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible and 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions require properly associated labels.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add a properly associated
findings.evidence:
<input
  type="password"
  value={password}
  onChange={e => setPassword(e.target.value)}
  placeholder="Your password"
  required
  autoFocus
  // No associated <label>
findings.impact: Screen reader users may not have clear context for the input field. Placeholder text disappears when the user starts typing, leaving sighted users without a label.

findings.title: Footer navigation links are generic and non-descriptive
findings.description: The footer navigation in src/app/layout.tsx (lines 101-108) contains multiple links with similar text like 'Contact' appearing twice with different destinations (line 104 as 'Contact' and line 108 as 'Talk to the Founder'), violating WCAG 2.4.4 Link Purpose. Keyboard-only users and screen reader users cannot distinguish between these links by text alone.
findings.fix_suggestion: Ensure all link text is unique or add aria-label for clarification: Contact Us ... Founder Contact Or use aria-label: Talk to the Founder
findings.evidence: Book a Pilot Demo Features Contact Trust & Security Privacy Accessibility Talk to the Founder
findings.impact: Users navigating via keyboard or screen reader cannot distinguish between the two 'Contact' links without examining the URL or being confused by the link text.

findings.title: Text color #7f879b (muted) may have insufficient contrast on dark backgrounds
findings.description: The CSS variable `--muted: #7f879b;` (defined in src/app/globals.css line 14) is used for secondary text throughout the application. When placed on the dark background (#0c0e14), the contrast ratio needs verification. WCAG 2.1 AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Verify contrast ratio and adjust --muted color if needed. Calculate #7f879b on #0c0e14:
- Approximate contrast ratio is around 5.8:1, which passes WCAG AA.
- However, if any use of --muted text is smaller than 14px bold (normal text), verify it meets 4.5:1 minimum.
- If contrast issues are found, lighten --muted to increase contrast (e.g., #8a93a8 or lighter).

Test with: https://webaim.org/resources/contrastchecker/
Input: Foreground #7f879b, Background #0c0e14
findings.impact: Users with low vision, color blindness, or viewing in bright conditions may have difficulty reading secondary text throughout the application.

findings.title: RecoveryCodesModal checkbox lacks associated label text
findings.description: The checkbox at src/components/RecoveryCodesModal.tsx (line 70-76) uses a label element with `htmlFor` properly set, but the checkbox itself is not clearly distinguished as required. The label text "I have saved these codes in a safe place" is clear, but there's no indication that this is a required action before the button becomes enabled.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aria-required and visual required indicator:
findings.evidence:
<label className="mb-4 flex items-center gap-2 text-sm text-muted">
  <input
    type="checkbox"
    checked={saved}
    onChange={e => setSaved(e.target.checked)}
    className="accent-accent"
  />
  I have saved these codes in a safe place
</label>
findings.impact: Users may not clearly understand that checking this box is mandatory to proceed, as there's no visual indicator of required status (like an asterisk or 'Required' text).

findings.title: Login form error messages may be missed by screen readers without aria-live
findings.description: The error message div in src/app/login/page.tsx (line 139-142) does not use `aria-live` to announce changes. While it's visible to sighted users, screen reader users may not be notified when an error appears.
findings.impact: Screen reader users navigating the form may not be immediately aware that an error occurred, especially if they don't reread the entire page.

findings.title: Spinner loading state lacks accessible label
findings.description: The loading spinner at src/app/login/page.tsx (lines 77-79) and similar instances throughout the codebase have no accessible name or aria-label. Screen reader users will not know what is loading.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aria-label or role="status" to the spinner:
findings.impact: Screen reader users will not know that the page is loading or waiting for a response.

findings.title: Button with only icon lacks proper aria-label (Close button in PageGuide)
findings.description: The close button in PageGuide at src/components/PageGuide.tsx (line 126-132) uses an X icon without visible text. While it has an aria-label, it should be verified that the label is clear and descriptive.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Add explicit focus styles:

<button
  onClick={close}
  className="absolute right-4 top-4 rounded-lg p-1 text-muted transition-colors hover:bg-surface-hover hover:text-foreground focus-visible:outline-2 focus-visible:outline-offset-2 focus-visible:outline-accent"
  aria-label="Close guide"
>
  <X size={18} />
</button>
findings.evidence:
<button
  onClick={close}
  className="absolute right-4 top-4 rounded-lg p-1 text-muted transition-colors hover:bg-surface-hover hover:text-foreground"
  aria-label="Close guide"
>
  <X size={18} />
</button>
findings.impact: Screen reader users will hear 'Close guide', which is clear. However, keyboard users may not have a visible focus indicator on this small icon button.

findings.title: Icon buttons in pagination may lack sufficient focus visibility
findings.description: The step indicator buttons in PageGuide at src/components/PageGuide.tsx (line 141-151) are small (h-2/w-2 px) and may not have sufficient focus indication for keyboard navigation.
findings.impact: Keyboard users may have difficulty seeing focus on these small buttons, especially if focus indicator is subtle.

findings.title: Form validation uses color alone to indicate errors
findings.description: In the register form and other forms, error messages are displayed with red text and red borders, but there's no other non-color indicator (like an asterisk, 'Error:', or icon) to convey the error status to users with color blindness.
findings.impact: Users with red color blindness may not recognize that an error occurred, violating WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color.

findings.title: Small close button on modals may lack sufficient focus indication
findings.description: The close button in RecoveryCodesModal and other modals may be too small (8px padding on icon) to show focus clearly. WCAG 2.4.7 requires visible focus indicators.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Ensure all buttons have clear focus styles:

className="w-full rounded-xl bg-accent px-4 py-3 font-semibold text-white transition-all hover:bg-accent-glow disabled:opacity-50 focus-visible:outline-2 focus-visible:outline-offset-2 focus-visible:outline-accent"
findings.evidence:
<button
  type="submit"
  disabled={loading || !password}
  className="w-full rounded-xl bg-accent px-4 py-3 font-semibold text-white transition-all hover:bg-accent-glow disabled:opacity-50"
>
findings.impact: Keyboard users may have difficulty seeing focus on buttons, especially in low-contrast scenarios or with vision impairments.

findings.title: Navigation menu button text changes with state (Open/Close) without aria-label change
findings.description: The menu button in Navigation.tsx (line 95-99) uses `aria-expanded` correctly, but the visible text also changes from 'Menu' to 'Close'. This is acceptable but could be clearer with consistent visible text and reliance on aria-expanded.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Consider using consistent aria-label that matches visible text:

aria-label={`${menuOpen ? 'Close' : 'Open'} navigation menu`}
findings.impact: Minor accessibility issue. The implementation is acceptable, but may cause slight confusion for screen reader users if visible text and aria-label diverge unexpectedly.

findings.title: Modal could benefit from aria-describedby for context
findings.description: The UnlockModal and RecoveryCodesModal could include `aria-describedby` to provide additional context to screen readers about the modal's purpose or content.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Add aria-describedby to the modal container:

Unlock Journal

Enter your password to unlock your encrypted journal entries.
findings.evidence: <div className="fixed inset-0 z-50 flex items-center justify-center bg-black/60 px-4">
findings.impact: This is an accessibility enhancement that would improve the experience for screen reader users, but is not a compliance failure.

findings.title: Consider adding breadcrumb navigation for event pages
findings.description: Event pages (e.g., /e/[slug]/agenda, /e/[slug]/directory) could benefit from breadcrumb navigation to help users understand their location within the site hierarchy, per WCAG 2.4.8 Location.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Consider adding breadcrumb navigation:
  1. Home
  2. /
  3. {event.eventName}
  4. /
  5. Agenda
findings.evidence: // Event route structure lacks breadcrumb
findings.impact: Users may have difficulty understanding their location in the page hierarchy, especially in deeply nested routes. This is an AAA enhancement, not a compliance failure.

findings.title: Custom tooltips using title attribute could be enhanced
findings.description: The DemoRoleSelector and other components use HTML `title` attributes for tooltips. While accessible, WCAG 1.4.13 suggests that custom tooltips should be dismissible and persistent. Native HTML title tooltips cannot be controlled by users.
findings.evidence: title={DEMO_ROLE_OPTIONS.find(option => option.role === r)?.summary}
findings.impact: This is an enhancement suggestion, not a compliance failure. Users relying on title attributes for context may miss information, but aria-label provides an alternative for screen readers.

findings.title: Missing aria-label on History Notes Textarea
findings.description: The textarea for adding notes to readings lacks an associated label or aria-label. Screen reader users cannot identify the purpose. This is a significant barrier for non-visual users.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aria-label attribute: <textarea aria-label="Add notes to this reading" ... />
findings.evidence: <textarea value={noteText} placeholder="Why is this reading meaningful to you?" rows={3} maxLength={500} />
findings.impact: Screen reader users cannot determine the textarea's purpose and must rely on context or placeholder text.

findings.title: Missing aria-label on Email Verification OTP Input
findings.description: The OTP input in the email verification banner lacks an explicit label or aria-label. Users cannot identify this as an email verification code input.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aria-label attribute: <input type="text" aria-label="Email verification code (6 digits)" ... />
findings.evidence: <input type="text" inputMode="numeric" pattern="[0-9]{6}" placeholder="6-digit code" autoComplete="one-time-code" />
findings.impact: Screen reader users cannot understand the purpose of this field. Critical authentication barrier for visually impaired users.

findings.title: Insufficient Color Contrast on Placeholder Text
findings.description: Placeholder text uses muted color at 50% opacity, resulting in low contrast ratio below 3:1 minimum on dark background. Users with low vision struggle to see placeholder text.
findings.fix_suggestion: Update placeholder color to full opacity: Change from 'placeholder:text-muted/50' to 'placeholder:text-muted' throughout codebase.
findings.evidence: --muted: #9590A8; Applied as placeholder:text-muted/50 throughout
findings.impact: Users with low vision cannot clearly see placeholder text, reducing usability of all form inputs.

findings.title: Missing aria-label on Unlock Modal Password Input
findings.description: The password input in UnlockModal lacks an aria-label. Users must rely on placeholder text which is insufficient.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aria-label attribute: <input type="password" aria-label="Enter password to unlock journal" ... />
findings.evidence: <input type="password" placeholder="Your password" required autoFocus />
findings.impact: Screen reader users cannot identify this as a password input for journal unlock.

findings.title: Required Field Indicators Not Text-Based
findings.description: Form fields use only HTML 'required' attribute but lack visible text indicators. Users cannot easily identify mandatory fields.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add visible required indicator:
findings.evidence: <input id="name" type="text" required autoComplete="name" className="..." />
findings.impact: Users cannot understand which fields are mandatory. Assistive technology users have no text-based indicator.

findings.title: Missing aria-label on Library Search Input
findings.description: The search input in card library lacks an aria-label. Cannot identify as library-specific search.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aria-label attribute: <input type="text" aria-label="Search card library by name or suit" ... />
findings.evidence: <input type="text" placeholder="Search..." value={searchQuery} onChange={(e) => setSearchQuery(e.target.value)} />
findings.impact: Screen reader users cannot understand this is a card library search.

findings.title: Missing aria-label on Library Personal Notes Textarea
findings.description: The textarea for personal notes on cards lacks an aria-label.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aria-label attribute: <textarea aria-label="Personal notes for this card" ... />
findings.evidence: <textarea value={cardNotes[card.id]} placeholder="Personal notes..." maxLength={500} />
findings.impact: Screen reader users cannot determine this is for personal card notes.

findings.title: Insufficient Touch Target Size on Modal Close Buttons
findings.description: Close buttons on modals use small padding (p-0.5 to p-1) with 16-20px icons, resulting in touch targets below 44x44px minimum.
findings.fix_suggestion: Increase padding on close buttons from p-0.5 to p-2: className="rounded-lg p-2 ..." with icon size={20}
findings.evidence: <button className="rounded-lg p-0.5 ..."><X size={16} /></button>
findings.impact: Mobile users may struggle to accurately tap small close buttons, particularly users with motor control difficulties.

findings.title: Generic Alt Text on Journal Attachment Image
findings.description: Journal entry images use generic alt text instead of descriptive content.
findings.fix_suggestion: Provide descriptive alt text: alt="User-uploaded journal reference image" or include context like date/entry association.
findings.impact: Screen reader users receive minimal information about uploaded images.

findings.title: Auto-Changing Form State Without Warning
findings.description: Some select/dropdown elements may change application state without explicit user confirmation or live region announcement.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aria-live="polite" feedback region for state changes, or require explicit button submission for major changes.
findings.evidence: Interpretation style selector, mood selector, and other onChange handlers that update state
findings.impact: Users of assistive technology may not expect or be aware of context changes from selecting options.

findings.title: Verify Dynamic Component ID Uniqueness
findings.file: Multiple component files with list rendering
findings.description: Components generating multiple elements should ensure ID attributes are unique when rendered in lists.
findings.fix_suggestion: Ensure dynamic IDs use unique identifiers: id={`mood-${mood}`} instead of reusing static IDs.
findings.evidence: Mood buttons, card grids, tag displays using .map()
findings.impact: Potential duplicate IDs could break aria-labelledby and aria-describedby references.

findings.title: Focus Order Not Explicitly Documented
findings.description: While focus management appears correct, there is no documentation of tab order strategy for complex interactions.
findings.fix_suggestion: Document focus strategy. Verify tab order follows visual left-to-right, top-to-bottom. Test with keyboard Tab key.
findings.evidence: Complex pages with spread layouts, multiple card reveals, interpretation toggles
findings.impact: Keyboard-only users may experience unexpected focus jumps on complex pages.

findings.title: AAA Aspiration: Enhanced Contrast Ratios
findings.description: Current AA compliance is met. AAA would require 7:1 normal text and 4.5:1 large text contrast.
findings.fix_suggestion: No action required for AA. Verify gold/accent colors (#d4a843) against background for AAA aspiration.
findings.evidence: Dark theme: #e8e0f0 on #0c0a1a ≈ 12:1 ratio (exceeds AA and AAA)
findings.impact: Aspiration: Increase readability for low-vision users beyond AA requirements.

findings.title: Strong ARIA and Semantic HTML Implementation
findings.description: Codebase demonstrates excellent implementation of accessibility standards.
findings.fix_suggestion: Continue current practices. Consider publishing accessibility guidelines for development team.
findings.evidence: Skip-to-main-content link with sr-only class, aria-live on toasts, proper semantic HTML
findings.impact: Positive: Strong accessibility foundation in place.

findings.title: Prefers-Reduced-Motion Properly Implemented
findings.description: Application correctly respects user motion preferences with comprehensive support.
findings.fix_suggestion: No action required. Continue this excellent practice.
findings.evidence: @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) with all animations disabled
findings.impact: Positive: Users with vestibular disorders are properly accommodated.
